title: Introduction to the nonprobsampling Package
text: nonprobsampling package provides methods correcting participation bias nonprobability samples using one multiple external probability reference surveys. package based generalized estimating equation framework proposed Landsman et al. (2026), pseudo-weights constructed aligning auxiliary information nonprobability sample corresponding information probability reference surveys. package implements three one-reference methods within framework: raking ratio calibration method Landsman et al. (2026), adjusted logistic propensity (ALP) method Wang et al. (2021), Chen–Li–Wu (CLW) method Chen et al. (2020). also implements multi-reference calibration method Landsman et al. (2026), integrates auxiliary information multiple reference surveys single reference survey contains variables relevant participation.

title: Introduction to the nonprobsampling Package
text: package includes three datasets derived real studies used throughout vignette illustrate estimation mean serum PSA (prostate-specific antigen) level among U.S. men aged 55 years older. dataset sc represents nonprobability sample contains outcome variable psa_level, defined serum PSA level (ng/mL). datasets sp1 sp2 probability reference surveys used estimate pseudo-weights. illustration purposes, shared variables already harmonized across example datasets. Variable names, factor levels, category definitions consistent. variables shared sc sp1 agecat, race, education, BMI, comorbidity, diabetes pros_enlarged. variables shared sc sp2 agecat, race, BMI, comorbidity, diabetes. user-supplied data, shared variables must harmonized estimation. Variable names must match exactly, continuous variables included participation model measured comparable scales across datasets.

title: Introduction to the nonprobsampling Package
text: calling est_pw(), probability reference survey must converted survey design object using either survey::svydesign() survey::svrepdesign(). objects retain sampling information needed design-based variance estimation. replicate weights example reference survey sp1_bootstrap generated using Rao-Wu rescaling bootstrap method (Rust Rao, 1996). provided illustrate variance estimation replicate-weight survey design object. real applications, users use replicate weights supplied survey data provider available.
code: ref1_design <- survey::svydesign(   ids     = ~psu_sp1,   strata  = ~strata_sp1,   weights = ~wts_sp1,   data    = sp1,   nest    = TRUE )  ref2_design <- survey::svydesign(   ids     = ~psu_sp2,   strata  = ~strata_sp2,   weights = ~wts_sp2,   data    = sp2,   nest    = TRUE ) ref1_design_rep_wts <- survey::svrepdesign(    data    = sp1_bootstrap,    weights = ~wts_sp1,    repweights = "bw[0-9]+",    type    = "bootstrap" )

title: Introduction to the nonprobsampling Package
text: est_pw() main function package. takes list first element nonprobability sample data frame remaining elements probability reference surveys represented survey design objects. method argument specifies pseudo-weighting method use, p_formula defines participation model selecting shared auxiliary variables used estimate pseudo-weights.

title: Introduction to the nonprobsampling Package
text: raking ratio calibration estimator one-reference pseudo-weighting method within generalized estimating equation framework. p_formula provided, constructed automatically variables shared nonprobability sample reference survey(s). generated formula shown model summary. title: Introduction to the nonprobsampling Package
text: two probability reference surveys available, use method = "multi" (leave method = NULL automatic selection). argument p_formula supplied list containing one participation model formula per reference survey. default, precali = TRUE applies cumulative precalibration pseudo-weight estimation. procedure, reference surveys sequentially calibrated previously processed surveys using overlapping variables p_formula. helps preserve information relationships overlapping survey-specific auxiliary variables within reference survey. argument sp_order controls order reference surveys processed cumulative precalibration pseudo-weight estimation. sp_order = "size" (default), surveys ordered largest smallest sample size, larger surveys used reference overlapping variables preferentially taken . sp_order = "given", surveys processed user-supplied order, may preferable particular reference survey considered reliable important.

what: Step 3 — Estimate Population Means and Prevalences
title: Introduction to the nonprobsampling Package
text: pseudo-weights estimated, pwmean() uses estimate population means numeric outcomes population prevalences observed category factor outcomes. cases, returned object includes unweighted pseudo-weighted estimates, standard errors, confidence intervals. numeric outcome, pwmean() estimates overall population mean. Since psa_level numeric, estimate reported mean. Binary 0/1 outcomes also treated numeric reported means. factor outcome, pwmean() estimates population prevalence observed category. , BMI factor outcome, output printed category prevalence labels.

title: Introduction to the nonprobsampling Package
text: nonprobsampling handles missing data two separate stages. First, est_pw() handles missing values participation model variables specified p_formula. default, est_pw() uses na.action = stats::na.omit, removes observations missing values estimating pseudo-weights. Alternatively, users may specify na.action = stats::na.exclude. case, excluded observations nonprobability sample retained returned objects, pseudo-weights set NA. useful users want keep original row alignment nonprobability sample later merging imputation. Second, pwmean() handles missing values outcome variable , specified, categorical domain variable. second layer missing-data handling applied pseudo-weights already estimated. illustration, create temporary versions example datasets. nonprobability sample, insert three missing values participation variable agecat four missing values outcome variable psa_level. reference survey sp1, insert two missing values participation variable education.
code: sc_with_na <- sc sc_with_na$agecat[c(1, 2, 4)] <- NA sc_with_na$psa_level[c(6, 7, 8, 9)] <- NA  sp1_with_na <- sp1 sp1_with_na$education[c(3, 4)] <- NA  ref1_design_with_na <- survey::svydesign(   ids     = ~psu_sp1,   strata  = ~strata_sp1,   weights = ~wts_sp1,   data    = sp1_with_na,   nest    = TRUE )

Observations excluded pseudo-weight estimation remain output, pseudo-weights recorded NA.

what: Missing-Data Handling in est_pw() with na.omit (Default)
title: Introduction to the nonprobsampling Package
text: Using na.action = stats::na.omit removes observations missing participation model variables returned objects. default behavior. Information excluded observations can obtained using S3 na.action() method. na.omit, returned pseudo-weight vector sc_updated contain observations used pseudo-weight estimation. summary, est_pw() handles missing values participation model variables, pwmean() handles missing values outcome domain variables. na.omit, excluded observations removed returned objects; na.exclude, original row structure preserved excluded observations receive NA pseudo-weights.

text: Inference Nonprobability Samples Using Multiple Reference Surveys. nonprobsampling implements pseudo-weighting methods finite population inference nonprobability samples, convenience samples, volunteer cohorts, opt-panels. participation mechanism nonprobability sample unknown, unadjusted estimates population means prevalences may biased. package addresses issue leveraging auxiliary information one multiple probability reference surveys estimate participation probabilities using inverses pseudo-weights obtain bias-corrected estimates finite population means prevalences. implemented methods based generalized estimating equations framework Landsman et al. (2026). includes multi-reference extension enables integration auxiliary information across multiple reference surveys.

title: Inference for Nonprobability Samples Using Multiple Reference Surveys
text: Install released version CRAN (available): Install development version GitHub:

title: Inference for Nonprobability Samples Using Multiple Reference Surveys
text: one reference survey: Calibration — raking ratio calibration (Landsman et al., 2026) ALP — adjusted logistic propensity weighting (Wang, Valliant, Li, 2021) CLW — Chen, Li, Wu (2020) multiple reference surveys: Multi-reference calibration — enables integration auxiliary information across multiple surveys single reference survey contains variables relevant participation (Landsman et al., 2026), optional cumulative precalibration step preserve information relationships overlapping unique auxiliary variables across reference surveys. Variance estimation based Taylor linearization, complex sampling designs reference surveys handled integration survey package; bootstrap replicate weights provided, bootstrap-based variance estimation also supported.

title: Inference for Nonprobability Samples Using Multiple Reference Surveys
text: Estimation proceeds two steps: est_pw() estimates pseudo-weights, pwmean() estimates pseudo-weighted mean prevalence outcome. multiple reference surveys, users provide one survey design object reference survey corresponding list participation model formulas, one formula specified survey. See vignette("nonprobsampling") details.
code: library(nonprobsampling)  data(sc)    # nonprobability sample (outcome: psa_level) data(sp1)   # probability reference survey  # Reference survey design ref1_design <- survey::svydesign(   ids     = ~psu_sp1,   strata  = ~strata_sp1,   weights = ~wts_sp1,   data    = sp1,   nest    = TRUE )  # Step 1: estimate pseudo-weights (one-reference calibration) fit <- est_pw(   data      = list(sc, ref1_design),   p_formula = ~ agecat + race + education + comorbidity + BMI + diabetes,   method    = "calibration",   control   = pw_solver_control(ftol = 1e-6) ) print(fit) summary(fit)  # Step 2: pseudo-weighted mean of the outcome, by BMI categories out <- pwmean(fit, y = "psa_level", zcol = "BMI") print(out) summary(out) data(sp2)   # second probability reference survey  ref2_design <- survey::svydesign(   ids     = ~psu_sp2,   strata  = ~strata_sp2,   weights = ~wts_sp2,   data    = sp2,   nest    = TRUE )  fit2 <- est_pw(   data = list(sc, ref1_design, ref2_design),   p_formula = list(     ~ agecat + race + education + psa_level + pros_enlarged + comorbidity,     ~ agecat + race + BMI + diabetes + comorbidity   ),   sp_order = "size",   precali = TRUE,   control = pw_solver_control(ftol = 1e-6) ) print(fit2) summary(fit2)

title: nonprobsampling: Inference for Nonprobability Samples Using Multiple Reference Surveys — nonprobsampling-package
text: package implements generalized estimating equation framework Landsman et al. (2026). Pseudo-weights obtained solving estimating equations equate auxiliary variable totals estimated nonprobability sample corresponding totals estimated one probability reference surveys. Totals nonprobability sample computed using estimated pseudo-weights, whereas totals reference surveys computed using known survey sampling weights. Several one reference methods arise special cases framework different choices weight estimating functions: raking ratio calibration method     Landsman et al. (2026); adjusted logistic propensity (ALP) method     Wang, Valliant, Li (2021); Chen–Li–Wu (CLW) method Chen, Li, Wu (2020). key feature package multi-reference extension calibration method. extension enables integration auxiliary information across multiple surveys single reference survey contains variables relevant participation (Landsman et al., 2026), optional cumulative precalibration step preserve information relationships overlapping unique auxiliary variables across reference surveys. Variance estimation based Taylor linearization. resulting analytic variance estimator accounts uncertainty pseudo-weight estimation sampling designs reference surveys integration survey package. package also supports bootstrap-based variance estimation bootstrap weights provided reference surveys.

title: nonprobsampling: Inference for Nonprobability Samples Using Multiple Reference Surveys — nonprobsampling-package
text: Estimation carried two steps: est_pw() estimates pseudo-weights using     nonprobability sample one reference survey design objects.     step fits participation model stores internal quantities     needed subsequent estimation. require outcome variable. pwmean() uses object returned est_pw()     estimate pseudo-weighted mean prevalence outcome, either     overall category. also returns standard error confidence     interval. Numerical settings solving estimating equations can specified pw_solver_control().

title: Control Solver Settings for Pseudo-Weight Estimation — pw_solver_control
text: solver Character string specifying numerical solver used solving estimating equations. Currently, "nleqslv" supported. Default "nleqslv". maxit Positive finite numeric value passed nleqslv::nleqslv() maximum number solver iterations. value converted integer stored returned control object. Default 150 global strategy specified (.e., global != "none"), 20 global strategy used (global = "none"), matching nleqslv's defaults. Since default global strategy "dbldog", effective default 150 unless global = "none" explicitly specified. trace Logical. TRUE, tracing solver progress information may requested underlying numerical routine supported. Default FALSE. method Character string specifying numerical method passed nleqslv::nleqslv(). Supported values "Newton" "Broyden". Default "Newton". global Character string specifying global strategy passed nleqslv::nleqslv(). Supported values "dbldog", "cline", "pwldog", "qline", "gline", "hook", "none". Default "dbldog". xscalm Character string specifying scaling method passed nleqslv::nleqslv(). Supported values "fixed" "auto". Default "fixed". ftol Positive finite numeric value passed nleqslv::nleqslv() function-value convergence tolerance. controls convergence based size estimating function. Default 1e-8. xtol Positive finite numeric value passed nleqslv::nleqslv() parameter-step convergence tolerance. controls convergence based changes parameter vector. Default 1e-8. nleqslv_control list additional control options passed nleqslv::nleqslv(). can include less commonly used control options, btol, cndtol, sigma, scalex. See nleqslv details.

title: Control Solver Settings for Pseudo-Weight Estimation — pw_solver_control
text: flat list containing solver control settings pseudo-weight estimation: solver selected numerical solver. method nleqslv numerical method. global nleqslv global strategy. xscalm nleqslv scaling method. ftol function-value convergence tolerance. xtol parameter-step convergence tolerance. maxit maximum number solver iterations, stored integer.     150 global strategy used; 20 global = "none".     Since default global strategy "dbldog", effective default     150 unless global = "none" explicitly specified. trace Logical value indicating whether tracing information requested. nleqslv_control list additional options passed     nleqslv::nleqslv().

title: Control Solver Settings for Pseudo-Weight Estimation — pw_solver_control
text: control object stores solver settings used pseudo-weight estimation step. passed est_pw control argument. Currently, solver = "nleqslv" supported. arguments method, global, xscalm, ftol, xtol, maxit correspond options used nleqslv::nleqslv(). collected internally passed nleqslv::nleqslv() pseudo-weight estimation step. argument ftol function-value convergence tolerance. controls convergence based size estimating function. argument xtol parameter-step convergence tolerance. controls convergence based changes parameter vector. argument maxit controls maximum number solver iterations. Additional, less commonly used nleqslv control options can supplied nleqslv_control. avoid ambiguity, supply ftol, xtol, maxit, trace inside nleqslv_control; use main arguments instead.

title: Control Solver Settings for Pseudo-Weight Estimation — pw_solver_control
code: ## Default solver control settings ctrl <- pw_solver_control()  ## Custom nleqslv solver settings ctrl <- pw_solver_control(   maxit  = 20,   trace  = FALSE,   method = "Newton",   global = "cline",   xscalm = "auto",   ftol   = 1e-8,   xtol   = 1e-10 )  ## Additional nleqslv control options ctrl <- pw_solver_control(   method = "Newton",   global = "dbldog",   nleqslv_control = list(     btol = 1e-3   ) )
